title: Quadlet - new unit type .login
body:
#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
Yes

```release-note
Quadlet - new unit for podman login
Quadlet - Add support for AuthFile to container and kube units
```

comments.body: [NON-BLOCKING] Packit jobs failed. @containers/packit-build please check. Everyone else, feel free to ignore.
@TomSweeneyRedHat I made the requested changes and added an example

[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *Luap99*, *ygalblum* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [Luap99,ygalblum] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
@containers/podman-maintainers PTAL

> Most importantly, podman login is not magic and AFAIK there’s no need at all to run it on every boot, or regularly. As long as the credentials work, the AuthFile never needs to change.

In many cases, the AuthFile is stored in a temporary FS and as a result logging in is required. Also note, this is not specific to boot time, it's upon demand.

> In particular, for deployments like that, it should not be necessary to make any requests to the registry (and therefore, it should not be necessary to deal with TLS configuration).

I don't know what you mean by "deployments like that"

> So why does a special login unit need to exist? Is it that Quadlet / auto-update finds it much easier to refer to systemd units than to mere files? (Something like that could well be the case, I am ignorant of the problem space. But then, fine, create a .login unit that only contains a parameter with an AuthFile path, and does not trigger any action.)

Yes, decoupling is a large part of it. But, your suggestion requires the user to do two things. This is even worse. BTW you could say the same about many other Quadlet units. Why do you need a `.network` unit? Networks persist so just create the network and reference it in the `.container` file. But, this is what Quadlet allows the users to do.

> Design—wise, I find the “special Cases If the name of the AuthFile ends with .login” generally worrisome and aesthetically unpleasant.

Quadlet users not only understand this design, they've learned to expect it. Cross referencing between Quadlet units is done this way all over. That's how `.network` units are resolved for the `Network` key, how `.volume` units are resolved for `Volume` and `Mount` key, etc.

> In many cases, the AuthFile is stored in a temporary FS and as a result logging in is required.

Are you saying that the primary source is in a temporary storage (in that case, it probably can _still_ use an auth-file format; just make a copy), or that the local system’s login state is in temporary storage (in that case, no need to _log in_ again; at worst, just make a copy of an AuthFile-formatted input file — or better, point at the authoritative persistent storage directly without _having_ a temporary-storage state)?

> why do you need a .network unit? Networks persist so just create the network and reference it in the .container file.

I think there needs to be a fundamental separation between sensitive-but-essentially-public-to-the-whole-company deployment configuration, and _secrets_; and it is irresponsible to design things that _recommend_ erasing that separation.

---

> > Design—wise, I find the “special Cases If the name of the AuthFile ends with .login” generally worrisome and aesthetically unpleasant.
> 
> Quadlet users not only understand this design, they've learned to expect it. Cross referencing between Quadlet units is done this way all over. That's how .network units are resolved for the Network key

\*shrug\* I still think it’s a bad idea, and the best time to stop with a bad idea is now.

But it might be more productive for me to wander away again and to let Quadlet-experienced maintainers review this.

The argument just use Authfile everywhere was my first impression too https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/26484#issuecomment-2996911889

I think it is a fair point to say we don't need this unit, a user just has to run login themselves and ship the authfile next to the quadlet files like they would do for the login unit so I guess yeah technically there is no strong requirement for this.

I am definitely totally ok with this approach here though I think there is one catch where this could be an advantage @mtrmac. If you set an authfile the password must be in clear text in said file on the persistent disk. With the login unit there is no strict requirement that the password is in clear text in said unit. The password could be passed as systemd creds (doesn't seem to work today due https://github.com/systemd/systemd/issues/31213) but I could see that working eventually or we ad the `sh -c ""` redirection ourselves. For such a case there could be a strong advantage in being able to share/deploy the quadlet files without the password in them and then the password can be deployed separately.

Maybe it would be good to have some more feedback on this here from the others.

> The password could be passed as systemd creds

Yes; at that point there would be a _benefit_ to adding a new feature.

[Well, better, the _whole authfile_ could be passed as systemd creds, couldn’t it? Quadlet might still want a separate unit for the loading of the auth file from systemd credentials, so that the feature does not have to be added to every single other Quadlet unit; \*shrug\*, I know ~nothing about Quadlet and I have no opinion on that.]

---

One general point to think about, “auth file” is really a much better point of abstraction than a “login” or a “password”, because an authfile can contain a whole set of credentials, e.g. for all mirrors. _And_ using an auth file ~automatically scopes the credentials only for the service that uses the auth file, without polluting global state like a `podman login` with no other options does: we can have images A and B from registries RA and RB, without giving the service that runs A credentials for RB.

> [Well, better, the whole authfile could be passed as systemd creds, couldn’t it? Quadlet might still want a separate unit for the loading of the auth file from systemd credentials, so that the feature does not have to be added to every single other Quadlet unit; *shrug*, I know ~nothing about Quadlet and I have no opinion on that.]

I think you are right and maybe it is even easier to do that. AFAIK systemd hands us a file so I would assume one could already just point the Authfile= key in each unit to that. As for sharing yeah I guess if we want to abstract then yes otherwise to is likely only two lines anyway.

> One general point to think about, “auth file” is really a much better point of abstraction than a “login” or a “password”, because an authfile can contain a whole set of credentials, e.g. for all mirrors. And using an auth file ~automatically scopes the credentials only for the service that uses the auth file, without polluting global state like a podman login with no other options does: we can have images A and B from registries RA and RB, without giving the service that runs A credentials for RB.

Yeah I guess that is a valid point. The login unit would not really work in the use case of needing two different credentials. So you convinced me, users should be providing the authfile themselves.

> Yeah I guess that is a valid point. The login unit would not really work in the use case of needing two different credentials. 

While this is true, it's relevant only in specific cases. For most cases, a single image is used. 
- `.container` is a single image. Yes, one can mount an image, but they can use a `.image` to define it
- `.image` pulls a single image
- `build` in most cases will use a single image. If needed users can use an `.image` file for more complicated cases.
- `.kube` similarly, complicated cases can use `.image` files

No one forces users to use a Quadlet file (or Quadlet at all, for that matter). It's about facilitating the different options. 

To me it's simpler to ship a `.login` file and tell the user to update the password in the file and from then on the depending service will just work as opposed to tell them to call login with `--authfile` pointing to a persistent place and update all the depending units to point to that place. 

In terms of security, both options are not really secure. In both cases the credentials are stored in a known place in plain text.

> While this is true, it's relevant only in specific cases. For most cases, a single image is used.

I would, conceptually, expect a single _service_ (a single “set of privileges) to share an AuthFile, for however many images, repositories, mirrors, or registries, are involved.

> To me it's simpler to ship a `.login` file

The way I think about it, shipping a `.login` file and shipping an AuthFile is exactly the same amount of work. Is that not the case?

> > To me it's simpler to ship a `.login` file
> 
> The way I think about it, shipping a `.login` file and shipping an AuthFile is exactly the same amount of work. Is that not the case?

yeah the more I think about this I agree. Also simple is relative on the target audience. Maybe some users would prefer it, hard to know? In terms of podman maintainer hat not having a feature in podman makes our lives easier so if there is no significant new value then maybe it is not the best to commit to supporting that long term.

> call login with --authfile pointing to a persistent place and update all the depending units to point to that place.

You can just all well update the authfile inplace, directly.

Just to be explicit, because I’m not 100% sure we are all talking about the same `login --authfile` process:
- at _configuration_ time, `podman login --authfile` is the right thing to do: it verifies the credentials, and it does the base64 encoding that user would otherwise need to do manually. (Or, in some hypothetical future, it might trigger an interactive authentication via a web browser, or something.)
- at _deployment_ time, `podman login --authfile` is unnecessary; do `podman pull --authfile` with the configuration-time-created file.

> shipping an AuthFile

Not sure I understand what you mean by this.
Let's say I have a containerized app. Users can gain access to the application only via my private repository. So, I provide them with a set of Quadlet files which they can download. I can't ship the AuthFile because each user has there own credentials. They will need to login to the registry.
So, without the `.login` file, I need to document how they need to log in while storing the auth file and update the dependant units. I also need to explain that is they use a simple `login` command, it will not persist after boot. Sure, it works. But more cumbersome

> You can just all well update the authfile inplace, directly.

I don't see anyone doing this.

1. find `$myApp.login`; enter the credentials; deploy the whole thing, see if the credentials were accepted
2. run `podman login --authfile $myApp.authfile`, immediately see whether the credentials work; deploy the whole thing with confidence.

I don’t see how 1. is really more convenient, but I am not working on operations and I wouldn’t know.

The difference that I see
- $myApp.login is part of the files you downloaded. 
- `--authfile $myApp.authfile` where is this file? How do I point the `.container` file to it? Alternatively, I tell the user to run with`--authfile /my-apps-specific-path/$myApp.authfile` and set the `.container` file in advance. But, then I also need the user to run `mkdir -p /my-apps-specific-path`

I think $myApp.authfile can be treated about the same as $myApp.login: ship a template file (well, an empty file would be best here). And use whatever distribution mechanism is in place to deploy the half-a-dozen `.container`/`.image` unit files to also deploy the `authfile` one.

Sure, the `.authfile` would be best placed in a different directory. In may way of thinking that’s a _feature_.

I think I'm missing something in what you're saying. How can one ship an authfile? It has to be created using the user's credentials. Maybe you're thinking about closed OS images (e.g. fleets or something like that)
I don’t know. How can one ship a `.login` file?

When I said "ship a `.login` file" I also said that the user will need to add their credentials into the file. But, all the rest of is ready made. That is, all other parameters are already there. In the `.login` file, the user does not need to know the URL of the registry, they don't need to care where the authfile is stored and don't need to point other units (e.g. `.container`) to it.

As you wrote the `.authfile` is an empty file and the user need to know where it is because they need to set the location in the login command. So, if they run `podman login --authfile $myApp.authfil` instead of `podman login --authfile $myApp.authfile`, yes they login successfully, but they store it in the wrong place and fail later.

I’m naive about these things.

In my mental model, all of those `.image` and `.container` files have some authoritative git repo, and Ansible automation to deploy them from that repo to the target machine. (Or, maybe, the application is a tarball of files and an `install.sh` script. Same thing.)

Users work in that git repo, or whatever it is. Paths on the final machine don’t matter, the deployment scripts handle that.

Or, alternatively, all the `.container` / … files are deployed directly to the target machine using RPM, and users are told that they must edit $path`.login` completely manually. Well, they would be told a different command.

In none of these situations are users _thinking_ about the paths at application deployment time. The paths are always a design-time concern, just like systemd unit names.

PR needs rebase.

<details>

Instructions for interacting with me using PR comments are available [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/pull-requests.md).  If you have questions or suggestions related to my behavior, please file an issue against the [kubernetes-sigs/prow](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/prow/issues/new?title=Prow%20issue:) repository.
</details>
Two minor nits.  Otherwise LGTM.  Sorry about the lag, just back from PTO.
And it looks like you need a rebase at this point to FWIW.
@TomSweeneyRedHat Have read the discussion here? The question is do we need to that at all or would it not be better to point user to authfiles directly.

@PhrozenByte How is creating/distributing a login file easier/better is your eyes than creating/distributing the authfile directly? Because that is what the argument is about here. You can already create a authfile once and ship it next to the quadlet file and refer to it in the container unit.

@Luap99 , I think the .login file is easier for end users to consume and digest. The @ygalblum users most likely do not care about podman and its command line arguments and they do not understand what the authfile even is. They want to run the containerized service the easiest way possible and the podman and quadlets are just implementation details.

The easiest way most likely is downloading the quadlets with instruction to "edit the .login file and change your username/password there". People are used to edit config files like that and there are much more users used to work with systemd than users used to work with podman (just guessing?)?

The authfile way is probably more complex, because the users have to run the "podman login --authfile". This is not the usual command people run and remember to re-run in case the username/pass changes and for @ygalblum , it is harder to find out sane "default path" which would work across different distributions.

Or is there some default path for authfiles which can be used already? If it's up to user to find out good place where to store the authfile, the user also has to change other systemd files to set the AuthFile to right location. This is yet another step. I think the workaround could be just using /opt/myapp/authfile and let the user run podman login --authfile  /opt/myapp/authfile. Not sure what prevents @ygalblum to do that.

Edit: There is definitely not a default authfile per systemd service :-).

@mtrmac is right that if there is some install.sh or ansible involved, nothing from that matter anymore most likely, because you can just script username/password or authfile creation and make it an implementation detail for a user as well.

I also like that this could in the future allow passing authfile as systemd credentials.

@Luap99 Multiple advantages actually:
- Right now it's impossible to persistently store credentials without prior setup ("prior setup" meaning to either add `--authfile` to every `run` and `auto-update` (which is particularly hard for unprivileged containers), or adding `REGISTRY_AUTH_FILE` to the global environment, see #12706, or writing custom scripts to run `login`)
- Auth files aren't in a "human writable format", but `.login` units are - i.e. one can't reasonably tell users to edit a JSON file due to its strict syntax, but editing a Systemd unit is generally safe
- Therefore one must create auth files on the command line; IMHO the whole point of Quadlet is to not require users to use and understand the command line
- Furthermore, it requires one to install Podman on the machine one is deploying the server from; might not be an issue at all, or a huge issue, depending on the setup, but requiring users to install Podman locally to setup a server isn't great
- Lastly, there's no well-known path for persistently storing auth files in; this is not as simple as documenting e.g. `/etc/containers/auth/`, because the path depends on whether one runs a rootful or unprivileged container, therefore making the `.container` units no longer agnostic of how the container is run
- `.login` units will get more powerful in the future, e.g. when adding support for Systemd credentials (could be as easy as adding a `--password-file` option to `login`), or when adding support for external secret providers (see #26488) that can then be utilised with `Secret=`

> * Right now it's impossible to persistently store credentials without prior setup ("prior setup" meaning to either add `--authfile` to every `run` and `auto-update`.

Adding a dependency on `.login` to every unit is exactly the same amount of work, isn't it?

> * Auth files aren't in a "human writable format", but `.login` units are - i.e. one can't reasonably tell users to edit a JSON file due to its strict syntax, but editing a Systemd unit is generally safe

That’s an anti-feature. E.g. the Docker Hub registry is, over time, slowly but seemingly purposefully, moving towards a model where authentication happens via OAuth, perhaps using a browser. Also, many of the cloud-specific registry services similarly require a cloud credential and a cloud-specific tool to log in.

It seems fairly unlikely that users editing files with static credentials will remain the default, or even _possible_, workflow, long-term. A tool will have to be involved.

> * Therefore one must create auth files on the command line; IMHO the whole point of Quadlet is to not require users to use and understand the command line

I don’t know how reading a man page and writing a file, vs. reading a man page and running a command, is substantially different, but I’ll defer to UI experts on this.

> * Furthermore, it requires one to install Podman on the machine one is deploying the server from

FWIW Skopeo would suffice, the `login` code path is ~the same — although that might not be any easier to deploy.

> * Lastly, there's no well-known path for persistently storing auth files in; this is not as simple as documenting e.g. `/etc/containers/auth/`, because the path depends on whether one runs a rootful or unprivileged container, therefore making the `.container` units no longer agnostic of how the container is run

Yes; I think that’s an interesting UI/design/workflow concern, and it would be _valuable_ to figure this out. (Not just for passphrases, but for any other files, e.g. configuration one might want to mount into a vendor-provided otherwise-unmodified container image.)

> * `.login` units will get more powerful in the future

The current design which contains a _single_ credential to be written to an exclusively-owned authfile is already _insufficient_ for use with mirrors.

> , e.g. when adding support for Systemd credentials (could be as easy as adding a `--password-file` option to `login`)

N/A. Just point `--authfile` at the systemd credential, no need to do anything else.

> , or when adding support for external secret providers (see [Idea: Load secrets from external providers #26488](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/26488)) that can then be utilised with `Secret=`

That could be a future feature _without_ ever providing a “create a systemd unit with a password in it” feature as proposed here.

> Adding a dependency on `.login` to every unit is exactly the same amount of work, isn't it?

Not necessarily: Systemd uses `.target` units as well-known synchronisation points. By adding `Before=some.target` to `.login` units and `After=some.target` to `.container` units one tells Systemd to start these units sequentially without requiring container-specific modifications. This can be a custom `containers.target`, or a more specific `http.target`, but also e.g. `default.target` (or `multi-user.target`).

> That’s an anti-feature. E.g. the Docker Hub registry is, over time, slowly but seemingly purposefully, moving towards a model where authentication happens via OAuth, perhaps using a browser. Also, many of the cloud-specific registry services similarly require a cloud credential and a cloud-specific tool to log in.
> 
> It seems fairly unlikely that users editing files with static credentials will remain the default, or even possible, workflow, long-term. A tool will have to be involved.

Can you please elaborate on how an OAuth workflow involving a browser is supposed to work on a server? I assume this is a misunderstanding: OAuth is solely used to get access tokens. Access tokens are still "static credentials".

If the access tokens are long-living, one simply deploys them to the server as before.

If they are short-living, we got a strong argument pro `.login` units: If authentication isn't as simple as a long-living static credential, one needs authentication providers like e.g. #26488. Deploying an auth file won't ever work then - one must rather let the server deal with getting that short-living access token and then run `login` with this static credential. Running `login` on the server is exactly what this `.login` unit is proposing?

> The current design which contains a single credential to be written to an exclusively-owned authfile is already *insufficient* for use with mirrors.

Please elaborate.

> N/A. Just point `--authfile` at the systemd credential, no need to do anything else.

systemd-creds is supposed to store credentials, not config files. A auth provider doesn't know Podman's auth file format, but just returns the credentials. It's up to `login` to bring these credentials into Podman's auth file format. That's exactly what this `.login` unit is proposing.

> That could be a future feature without ever providing a “create a systemd unit with a password in it” feature as proposed here.

I'm confused. You vote for not introducing a `.login` unit and therefore not running `login` on the server, but to deploy client-generated auth files instead. Please elaborate on how these future features could be implemented without running `login` on the server?

Or do you solely oppose the `Password=` directive? What's the issue with that?

> > Adding a dependency on `.login` to every unit is exactly the same amount of work, isn't it?
> 
> Not necessarily: Systemd uses `.target` units as well-known synchronisation points. By adding `Before=some.target` to `.login` units and `After=some.target` to `.container` units …

I meant adding `Authfile: /some/path` vs. `Authfile: some-unit.login`. Exactly one line to add to every consumer unit in both cases.

> > That’s an anti-feature. E.g. the Docker Hub registry is, over time, slowly but seemingly purposefully, moving towards a model where authentication happens via OAuth, perhaps using a browser. Also, many of the cloud-specific registry services similarly require a cloud credential and a cloud-specific tool to log in.
> > It seems fairly unlikely that users editing files with static credentials will remain the default, or even possible, workflow, long-term. A tool will have to be involved.
> 
> Can you please elaborate on how an OAuth workflow involving a browser is supposed to work on a server? I assume this is a misunderstanding: OAuth is solely used to get access tokens. Access tokens are still "static credentials".

Yes, but explicitly created using a _tool_, perhaps using a sequence of HTTP requests. So the workflow is `login-using-a-tool --out=authfile.json` and then use the file as is, vs. `login-using-a-tool --out=some-temporary-authfile.json` and then manually copy&paste the now-static credential from one file to another. The manual edits are just extra work.

> > The current design which contains a single credential to be written to an exclusively-owned authfile is already _insufficient_ for use with mirrors.
> 
> Please elaborate.

How do I create an `authfile` that contains credentials for the primary + 2 mirrors? The current `login` syntax supports _one_ password.

(Yes, in that other thread there is a discussion about using multiple `.login` units and telling users to _manually_ order them in sequence. Manual and error-prone work compared to pre-creating a single file.)

> > That could be a future feature without ever providing a “create a systemd unit with a password in it” feature as proposed here.
> 
> I'm confused. You vote for not introducing a `.login` unit and therefore not running `login` on the server, but to deploy client-generated auth files instead.

I’m confused about which computer is the "client" and which one is the "server" here. On which one are the Quadlet units deployed? On which one are they set up / maintained / edited, to be deployed to the other one?

> Or do you solely oppose the `Password=` directive?

That one I oppose strongly, and I wrote why (separation of secrets vs. sensitive-but-not-secret configuration). And overall I think the “one credential is necessary” mental model is incorrect, but that’s a weaker objection.

> I’m confused about which computer is the "client" and which one is the "server" here. On which one are the Quadlet units deployed? On which one are they set up / maintained / edited, to be deployed to the other one?

Let's call "server" the machine the Quadlet units are deployed to, and "client" the machine that is responsible for preparing and deploying the config to the "server".

> Yes, but explicitly created using a tool, perhaps using a sequence of HTTP requests. So the workflow is `login-using-a-tool --out=authfile.json` and then use the file as is, vs. `login-using-a-tool --out=some-temporary-authfile.json` and then manually copy&paste the now-static credential from one file to another. The manual edits are just extra work.

First, I don't think that `login-using-a-tool` would ever directly write to a Podman auth file, because Podman auth files are in no way an established file format for credentials. So, in reality it's likely more like `login-using-a-tool | podman login --authfile=authfile.json --password-stdin`.

Second, the whole idea of `.login` units is to *not* run `login` on the client, but on the server. When assuming short-living OAuth access tokens, one simply can't run `login-using-a-tool` on the client, but must run it on the server. How does your approach work without running `login` on the server?

So, in the end it's more like running `login-using-a-tool | podman login --authfile=authfile.json --password-stdin` on the client and then deploying `auth.json` to the server vs. just running `login-using-a-tool | podman login --authfile=authfile.json --password-stdin` on the server.

> How do I create an authfile that contains credentials for the primary + 2 mirrors? The current login syntax supports one password.
> 
> (Yes, in that other thread there is a discussion about using multiple .login units and telling users to manually order them in sequence. Manual and error-prone work compared to pre-creating a single file.)

By using three `.login` units. From what I see from the outside how Quadlet works (I don't know the code, @ygalblum WDYT?), I think that it's possible to let Quadlet order `.login` units to not run into race conditions, see https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/26507#discussion_r2219028792

> > Or do you solely oppose the `Password=` directive?
> 
> That one I oppose strongly, and I wrote why (separation of secrets vs. sensitive-but-not-secret configuration).

I honestly don't see much of a difference whether one deploys a `auth.json` with appropriate file permissions, or a `.login` unit with appropriate file permissions in this regard. From my experience it's rather rare for applications to store static credentials in separate files - and if they do, it's usually more a implementation detail than a design decision. I consider this more a personal preference - admittedly, as RH employee your personal preference is more important than mine :wink: Or, are there any hard facts I miss, like security implications?

> And overall I think the “one credential is necessary” mental model is incorrect, but that’s a weaker objection.

I honestly still don't understand how that opposes running `login` on the server. This is only true when using the `Password=` directive. The `.login` unit doesn't just consist of the `Password=` directive.

> By using three .login units. From what I see from the outside how Quadlet works (I don't know the code, @ygalblum WDYT?), I think that it's possible to let Quadlet order .login units to not run into race conditions, see https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/26507#discussion_r2219028792

I don't think Quadlet can do it on its own. How will it know the order? Or do you mean that the order itself does not matter as long as they are not executed in parallel?

> > By using three .login units. From what I see from the outside how Quadlet works (I don't know the code, @ygalblum WDYT?), I think that it's possible to let Quadlet order .login units to not run into race conditions, see [#26507 (comment)](https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/26507#discussion_r2219028792)
> 
> I don't think Quadlet can do it on its own. How will it know the order? Or do you mean that the order itself does not matter as long as they are not executed in parallel?

The latter.

Order doesn't matter, only that multiple `login` processes don't attempt to write to the same file in parallel. Multiple `login` processes can run in parallel as long as they write to different files though.

I don't know Go, but in other languages I'd simply group the `.login` units by `AuthFile=` (including a "no `AuthFile=` group") and then add `After=` directives to the units referencing their respective previous `.login` unit within that same group. This way all but the first `.login` unit in a group gets an `After=` directive. Two `.login` units writing to e.g. `AuthFile=/etc/containers/auth/private-repo.json` then run sequentially, but `.login` units writing to different files (e.g. `AuthFile=/etc/containers/auth/other-repo.json` and `AuthFile=/etc/containers/auth/yet-another-repo.json`) still run in parallel.

> The latter.

I see. It will require some additional code in the way Quadlet handles the dependency between files. But, it should be doable.

Having said that, I want to add that before I make any changes I need to get an approval on the premise of this feature. No point for me to continue working on this if it will never get approved

> > Yes, but explicitly created using a tool, perhaps using a sequence of HTTP requests. So the workflow is `login-using-a-tool --out=authfile.json` and then use the file as is, vs. `login-using-a-tool --out=some-temporary-authfile.json` and then manually copy&paste the now-static credential from one file to another. The manual edits are just extra work.
> 
> First, I don't think that `login-using-a-tool` would ever directly write to a Podman auth file, because Podman auth files are in no way an established file format for credentials. So, in reality it's likely more like `login-using-a-tool | podman login --authfile=authfile.json --password-stdin`.

The files (well, “credentials in `.docker/config.json`) _are_ actually somewhat widely used as a file format. See `gcloud auth configure-docker`, `az acr login`; AWS ECR similarly recommends configuring the file to use a helper, not using a password (although piping a password/token _is_ an option, to be fair).
- there is an `identityToken` field in the files, as an explicit alternative to a password. That is designed to be entirely tool-managed, `podman login` will refuse to do anything.

> Second, the whole idea of `.login` units is to _not_ run `login` on the client, but on the server. When assuming short-living OAuth access tokens, one simply can't run `login-using-a-tool` on the client, but must run it on the server. How does your approach work without running `login` on the server?

- That would be an interesting discussion for `.login` items that are designed to implement regular OAuth re-authentications; not for the `.login` items proposed in _this_ PR.
- If the “server” should be running regular OAuth re-authentications from a single static credential located on that server… that seems pointless, the server still has a long-lasting credential. The hypothesis of such a feature is problematic (admitting that some registry operators’ policies might force “servers” do do such a thing).

> > And overall I think the “one credential is necessary” mental model is incorrect, but that’s a weaker objection.
> 
> I honestly still don't understand how that opposes running `login` on the server. 

When running “login” on the server is _unnecessary_, to me that’s already a pretty strong reason not to design workflows that require or recommend it. Not a decisive reason, but there better be very significant benefits to designing things that way.

Personally I find the idea that, to support 3 mirrors, I must create 3 separate `.login` items that target the same file, and manually order them to avoid conflicts (well, this is a feature that should be fixed anyway), _and_ create a dependency on all 3 items in every single consumer … way too much hassle to be elegant. I can see an argument for a philosophy where “simple things are done using one simple way, and more complex things are done using a completely different more complex say”… aesthetically I don’t prefer that kind of an approach, but that _is_ really up to Quadlet maintainers.

> > Second, the whole idea of `.login` units is to _not_ run `login` on the client, but on the server. When assuming short-living OAuth access tokens, one simply can't run `login-using-a-tool` on the client, but must run it on the server. How does your approach work without running `login` on the server?
> 
> That would be an interesting discussion for `.login` items that are designed to implement regular OAuth re-authentications; not for the `.login` items proposed in _this_ PR.

I don't really see a reason why one would want to add `login`-only secret providers, given that there's already #26488 and `Secret=` in `.login` units (resp. `login --secret`). Anyway, you're right, that's discussed elsewhere.

> If the “server” should be running regular OAuth re-authentications from a single static credential located on that server… that seems pointless, the server still has a long-lasting credential. The hypothesis of such a feature is problematic (admitting that some registry operators’ policies might force “servers” do do such a thing).

Think about e.g. TPMs. But that's not the point. The point is that running `login` on the server is more flexible, because there are use-cases in which one simply can't run `login` on the client.

> > > And overall I think the “one credential is necessary” mental model is incorrect, but that’s a weaker objection.
> > 
> > 
> > I honestly still don't understand how that opposes running `login` on the server.
> 
> When running “login” on the server is _unnecessary_, to me that’s already a pretty strong reason not to design workflows that require or recommend it. Not a decisive reason, but there better be very significant benefits to designing things that way.

My reason is exactly the same, just the other way round: When running `login` on the client is _unnecessary_, that's […].

Again, I might not even have Podman installed on the machine that deploys my servers (the "client"). Requiring users to install Podman there even though they simply don't need it is a bad thing and anything but "simple".

> Personally I find the idea that, to support 3 mirrors, I must create 3 separate `.login` items that target the same file, and manually order them to avoid conflicts (well, this is a feature that should be fixed anyway), _and_ create a dependency on all 3 items in every single consumer … way too much hassle to be elegant.

We've already discussed this, you don't have to do either one. Please see above.

Besides, adding a `.login` unit doesn't harm your workflow in any way. You can keep on doing it that way if that's simpler for you.

Running `login` on the server solves a whole bunch of other issues, including my personal issue with Podman not being able to use persistently stored credentials without either `--authfile` or `REGISTRY_AUTH_FILE` (i.e. #12706, https://github.com/containers/image/pull/1746, https://github.com/containers/image/pull/2600, and https://github.com/containers/container-libs/issues/196) - which are anything but "simple". Even the most basic use cases like authenticating against ghcr.io to pull images from private repos is unnecessary hard right now. Deploying a single `.login` unit that runs before any `.container` unit solves that. You simply can't do that with an auth file alone. You either need to modify any Podman command to add `--authfile`, or add configs to modify the global environment to include `REGISTRY_AUTH_FILE`. That's undoubtedly harder.

> Deploying a single `.login` unit that runs before any `.container` unit solves that.

If you mean that the `.login` unit would edit `root`’s per-interactive-user private state… I think making that very hard is yet another good reason _not_ to support `.login`. Per-user state is per-user state and not per-system state. Most systemd units, even if running as root, have no business reading root’s per-user state.

\*sigh\* maybe I really should just let this be and let people who like this kind of stuff build Quadlet how they like?

> Personally I find the idea that, to support 3 mirrors, I must create 3 separate .login items that target the same file, and manually order them to avoid conflicts (well, this is a feature that should be fixed anyway), and create a dependency on all 3 items in every single consumer … way too much hassle to be elegant. I can see an argument for a philosophy where “simple things are done using one simple way, and more complex things are done using a completely different more complex say”… aesthetically I don’t prefer that kind of an approach, but that is really up to Quadlet maintainers.

First, as you mentioned, the only reason Quadlet would need to maintain an order is because of a bug in `podman login`. 

Second, I guess I hold the opposite view. Solve something for the 80% and let the 20% worry about their complex issues. For sure, I wouldn't block the solution for the 80% because it doesn't fit all the different options. 

> the `Password=` directive

Quadlet is not about deciding for users how they should use podman commands (we tried that in the beginning and ended up removing almost all opinions). If users want to use this directive, they should be able to do that along with the consequences.
A friendly reminder that this PR had no activity for 30 days.
Closing this PR following feedback from the containers team. Can be revisited later if there's any interest.
